skill.name: requirements-elicitation
skill.description: Requirements gathering and elicitation techniques for business analysis. Use when conducting stakeholder interviews, gathering functional/non-functional requirements, or identifying gaps in requirements.
skill.summary: Requirements gathering and elicitation techniques for business analysis. Use when conducting stakeho...
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged possible C2 keywords, weak cryptography, and Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found these are false positives from SOC2 text, words containing des, Markdown code fences, and example formatting. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it can use Read and Write tools for requirements documents.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Tool Access
low_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Grep, Glob, and Write tools. This is consistent with document analysis and requirements artifact creation, but users should review generated files before accepting changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration directly lists file read, search, and write capabilities. No evidence found of malicious file access instructions.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Dismissed as Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences and example formatting, not Ruby or shell execution. The flagged weak cryptography hits are substrings in ordinary prose, and the C2 hit is SOC2 compliance text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible Markdown guidance and templates, with no executable language or command invocation. The SOC2 and des substring matches explain the high-risk static alerts.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss key requirements because interviews lack structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through stakeholder questions, gap analysis, prioritization, and validation.
actual_capabilities: Provides structured stakeholder interview techniques for business and technical roles.
Separates functional requirements, non-functional requirements, constraints, and assumptions.
Uses FURPS+, SMART, INVEST, and MoSCoW methods for requirements quality and priority checks.
Supports greenfield, brownfield, integration, migration, and validation question flows.
Formats outputs as requirements lists, user stories, acceptance criteria, and traceability links.
limitations: It does not replace stakeholder approval or domain expert review.
It cannot verify business facts unless users provide source material.
It does not estimate delivery effort beyond basic value and effort framing.
It may write draft artifacts only when the host tool permits file writing.

use_cases.title: Plan stakeholder interviews
use_cases.description: Prepare role-specific questions that cover scope, workflows, rules, data, security, and validation.

use_cases.title: Convert discovery notes into requirements
use_cases.description: Organize notes into functional requirements, non-functional requirements, assumptions, constraints, and open questions.

use_cases.title: Clarify implementation-ready stories
use_cases.description: Turn confirmed needs into user stories with acceptance criteria and traceability to business goals.

prompt_templates.title: Start a requirements interview
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a stakeholder interview for a new software project. Ask questions about scope, users, workflows, data, constraints, and success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze existing notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these discovery notes and classify them into functional requirements, non-functional requirements, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Find requirement gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the current-state notes with the desired future state. Identify gaps, missing stakeholders, unresolved decisions, and follow-up questions.

prompt_templates.title: Create prioritized delivery artifacts
prompt_templates.prompt: Transform the confirmed requirements into prioritized user stories with acceptance criteria, MoSCoW priority, traceability, and validation questions for stakeholders.

output_examples.input: A product team needs requirements for a customer portal.
output_examples.output: A structured interview guide covering user roles, core workflows, account data, authentication, reporting, support needs, and success metrics.

output_examples.input: Discovery notes mention slow reports, unclear approvals, and compliance needs.
output_examples.output: A requirements summary with performance targets, approval workflow questions, audit logging needs, constraints, and stakeholder validation items.

output_examples.input: A legacy system must be replaced without disrupting operations.
output_examples.output: A brownfield gap analysis covering current capabilities, migration data, parallel run options, cutover risks, rollback needs, and training requirements.

best_practices: Start with open-ended context questions before asking detailed validation questions.
Separate facts, assumptions, decisions, risks, and unresolved questions in every artifact.
Validate requirements with stakeholders before turning them into delivery commitments.
anti_patterns: Do not treat one stakeholder view as the complete source of truth.
Do not mix solution design choices into requirements without labeling them as constraints.
Do not skip non-functional requirements such as security, reliability, usability, and supportability.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create interview guides, requirement lists, user stories, acceptance criteria, gap analyses, and traceability outlines.

faq.question: Can it support both new and existing systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes greenfield questions, brownfield questions, migration prompts, integration prompts, and validation patterns.

faq.question: Does it make final product decisions?
faq.answer: No. It structures analysis and questions, but stakeholders must confirm decisions, priorities, and tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it handle non-functional requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers performance, reliability, usability, security, supportability, constraints, and measurable quality checks.

faq.question: Is this only for business analysts?
faq.answer: No. Product managers, engineering leads, consultants, and delivery teams can use it during discovery and planning.

faq.question: Does it require code access?
faq.answer: No. It works from stakeholder input and documents. Code access is only useful when analyzing an existing system.
